title: Gel marbling — marbling in a gelatin bath
materials: gelatin-leaf (or gelatin-size: 1 tablespoon powdered gelatin per 500ml water)
sources: Gelatin-bath marbling predates carrageenan; documented in Josef Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (1893, English translation 1894) — public domain. Halfer notes gelatin as an early size before carrageenan became dominant.
notes: Marbling in a gelatin bath instead of carrageenan. Gelatin produces a slightly firmer size, meaning pigments spread less readily but hold pattern detail more crisply. Good for non-combed patterns (stone, suminagashi-style concentric rings). Covers: preparing the gelatin bath (bloom in cold water, dissolve in warm, let cool to near-room-temperature before use — must not be warm when marbling), reducing surface tension with ox-gall, applying pigment in drops, the different spread behaviour vs carrageenan (tighter spread, higher ridges), laying the paper, lifting, rinsing, drying. Note: gelatin bath has a shorter working life than carrageenan — use same-day.
